explanation_md: Base classes are constructed in the order they appear in the base-class list: `Flyable` first, then `Swimmable`. The derived class constructor body runs last. This mirrors the single-inheritance rule: base before derived.

options.a: `C` cannot inherit from both `A` and `B` because they define functions with the same name
options.b: `c.hello()` is ambiguous — both `A::hello` and `B::hello` are equally viable; use `c.A::hello()` to disambiguate
options.c: `hello` is private in `C` because it is inherited from two bases
options.d: Multiple inheritance requires `virtual` on at least one base class

explanation_md: When two base classes both provide a member with the same name and neither is a better match, the call is ambiguous and the compiler reports an error. The fix is to qualify the call: `c.A::hello()` or `c.B::hello()`. Alternatively, `C` can define its own `hello()` that delegates to the desired base.

prompt_md: Class `RoboDog` inherits from both `Dog` and `Robot`, which both inherit from `Animal` (normal, non-virtual inheritance). How many copies of `Animal`'s data members does `RoboDog` contain?

options.a: Zero — `Animal` data is shared between `Dog` and `Robot` automatically
options.b: One — C++ merges duplicate base data automatically
options.c: Two — one from `Dog` and one from `Robot`
options.d: It depends on whether the bases are declared `public` or `protected`

explanation_md: Without virtual inheritance, each path through the inheritance chain brings its own full copy of the shared base. `Dog` has an `Animal` subobject, and `Robot` has a separate `Animal` subobject. `RoboDog` therefore contains two copies. This is the diamond problem. Virtual inheritance (`virtual public Animal`) merges them into one shared subobject.

prompt_md: With virtual inheritance, which class is responsible for constructing the virtual base?

options.a: The first direct base listed in the derived class declaration
options.b: Each direct base constructs the virtual base independently
options.c: The most-derived class is responsible; intermediate base constructors' calls to the virtual base are ignored
options.d: The virtual base constructs itself with a default constructor regardless of anything the derived class says

explanation_md: With virtual inheritance, the most-derived concrete class in the chain is responsible for constructing the virtual base. Any constructor calls to the virtual base inside intermediate classes (`Dog`, `Robot`) are silently skipped when a more-derived constructor is running. The most-derived class must provide the arguments for the virtual base's constructor, or the default constructor is used.
`c.A::hello()` explicitly calls `A`'s version, printing "A". Then `greetBoth()` calls `A::hello()` (prints "A") followed by `B::hello()` (prints "B"). Total: three lines.
